critiques.summary: Probed the staged Set 071 S3 docs/release changes against the actual repo state and found two blocking cross-artifact inaccuracies: the docs claim the set is already closed/verified even though session-state still shows Session 3 in progress, and they claim a required path-aware-critique artifact exists even though no such file is present in the set directory. These are material because they misstate close-out readiness and would mislead the gate/operator.

findings.description: Violation: the new docs repeatedly claim the set is already complete, e.g. `change-log.md` says `Session 3 of 3 -- Synthesis + docs + staged release + dogfood + close (FINAL)` with `Status: CLOSED, VERIFIED`, and its intro says the release metadata + changelog `are committed`. Impact: this is a merge-blocking factual error about the current repository state. A future maintainer or operator reading the set record would believe close-out already happened and that the set is in a shippable terminal state, when the live lifecycle files still show an open session. That directly changes the close/merge decision for this set. Evidence: `docs/session-sets/071-verifier-materiality-and-nitpick-discipline/session-state.json` still has top-level `status: "in-progress"` and Session 3 with `status: "in-progress"` / `completedAt: null`; the changed `activity-log.json` likewise ends at an S3 verification entry and does not record a terminal close. Fix: either run the real close-out and update the lifecycle artifacts before landing these docs, or rewrite the docs/change log to describe S3 as staged/in progress rather than closed.

Violation: the new docs claim the required path-aware critique artifact already exists, e.g. `change-log.md` says `the final round kept as the gate artifact path-aware-critique.json` and the end-of-set table lists `This change log + dogfood artifact | change-log.md, path-aware-critique.json | S3`. Impact: on a `pathAwareCritique: required` set this is merge-blocking completeness drift, because it tells the operator/gate that the close-out prerequisite is satisfied when the artifact is not actually present. If shipped as-is, the set narrative asserts a completed required deliverable that the close-out gate cannot validate. Evidence: `activity-log.json` records `path_aware_critique` choice `required`, but `list_dir(docs/session-sets/071-verifier-materiality-and-nitpick-discipline)` shows no `path-aware-critique.json` file at all. Fix: generate and save the real multi-provider artifact before claiming it exists, or remove/rephrase the claims until the artifact is actually written.

critiques.summary:
I have reviewed the core logic, tests, integration, and documentation for the verifier materiality gate and re-verify loop discipline. The implementation is correct, complete, and internally consistent with the design described in the prompt. I found no material defects.

I read `ai_router/verification.py` and confirmed that `parse_verification_response` is hardened against the claimed parsing bugs (e.g., `VERDICT:` prefix, markdown-bold severity, header self-match). I verified that it correctly implements the adjudicated design decision to trust a `VERIFIED` token from the push surface to avoid churn, explicitly returning no findings in that case. I also confirmed that `is_blocking_verdict` implements the severity-derived logic correctly, where a Critical/Major finding (or an unknown severity) blocks regardless of the verdict token, while an `ISSUES_FOUND` with only `Minor` findings is correctly classified as non-blocking.

I inspected `ai_router/__init__.py` and confirmed that the `VerificationResult` dataclass is updated with the `blocking` and `nits` fields, and that these are correctly populated within the `_run_verification` function by calling the new predicates.

I reviewed the new test file, `ai_router/tests/test_blocking_classifier.py`, and found it to be comprehensive. It includes a full test matrix for `is_blocking_verdict`, specific regression tests for the parsing bugs, and end-to-end tests confirming that Minor-only findings result in a non-blocking classification.

Finally, I checked `docs/ai-led-session-workflow.md` and verified that the new "Materiality and the re-verify loop discipline" section accurately describes the new code behavior, correctly instructing users that the blocking decision is derived from severity via `is_blocking_verdict`, not from the bare verdict token. All artifacts are in alignment.
